1: vayyehi kishmo'a kol malkei ha'Emori asher be'ever hayarden yammah vekhol malkei hakeana'an asher al hayam et asher hovish YHWH et mei hayarden mipnei vnei Yisrael — the news of the Jordan crossing reaches the Canaanite and Amorite kings. Vayyimmas levavam — "their hearts melted" — mamas (the verb also used in Joshua 2:11). Velo hayah vahem ruach od mipnei vnei Yisrael — "there was no spirit left in them because of the sons of Israel." Rahab's description of Canaanite morale (Joshua 2:9-11) is confirmed as the condition of all the kings — the divine terror has preceded Israel's army.
2: ba'et hahee amar YHWH el Yehoshua asah lekha charvot tsurim — "at that time the LORD said to Joshua: make flint knives." Charvot tsurim — flint (literally "rock") knives. The use of flint rather than metal may reflect the archaic character of the circumcision rite (cf. Zipporah in Exodus 4:25 using a flint). Vemuol shuv et bnei Yisrael shenit — "circumcise the sons of Israel again, a second time." The phrasing is unusual: not a second circumcision of individuals but a circumcision of the next generation, which is in this sense a second national circumcision.
3: Giv'at ha'aralot — "the hill of foreskins." The place name is etiology: the site is named for the rite performed there. Flint-knife circumcision at the threshold of the conquest is both a covenant renewal and a commissioning: Israel enters battle as a circumcised (covenanted) people. The mass circumcision at Gilgal before any military action makes the point that covenant identity precedes military effectiveness.
4: vezeh hadavar asher mal Yehoshua — "and this is the reason why Joshua circumcised them." The explanatory pericope of Joshua 5:4-7 is an anachronistic aside within the narrative. All the men who came out of Egypt and had been circumcised had died in the wilderness — the entire Sinai generation. The wilderness wandering had suspended circumcision practice for the second generation.
5: kol ha'am hayotsei nimolu vekhol ha'am hayyullud bamidbar baderekh betzetam mimitsrayim lo malu — "all the people who came out had been circumcised, but all the people born in the wilderness on the journey had not been circumcised." The legal and covenantal problem is stated precisely: there is a generation of Israelite men who bear the identity of Israel but not its sign. The conquest cannot proceed with an uncircumcised army — covenant integrity must precede covenant action.
6: ki arba'im shanah halekhu vnei Yisrael bamidbar — "the sons of Israel walked in the wilderness forty years." The reason for the wilderness-period suspension of circumcision is the judgment of Joshua 14:29-34 — those men would die in the wilderness; their sons would enter the land. Asher lo sheme'u beqol YHWH — "who did not listen to the voice of the LORD" — the parental disobedience caused the children's covenantal gap. The forty years is the measure of the judgment on unbelief.
7: ve'et bnehem heqim tachtam otam mal Yehoshua ki arelim hayu — "and their children he circumcised in their place, for they were uncircumcised." Arelim (uncircumcised) in Hebrew has a range: literally foreskinned, but metaphorically it describes anything closed, blocked, or unresponsive — uncircumcised lips (Exodus 6:12), uncircumcised heart (Jeremiah 9:26). The mass circumcision at Gilgal removes the literal condition; the prophets call for the heart-circumcision that the literal rite signifies (Deuteronomy 10:16; Jeremiah 4:4; cf. Colossians 2:11).
8: vayyihyu veshivtam tachtam bamachaneh ad cheyotam — "they remained in camp until they had healed." Chayotam from chayah — to live, recover, be restored to health. The verb carries the connotation of reviving. The period of healing in the camp is one of Israel's few moments of military vulnerability in the conquest narrative — no fighting while circumcised and recovering — an act of faith that God would protect them during their incapacity.
9: vayyomer YHWH el Yehoshua hayyom galoti et cherpat Mitsrayim me'aleikhem — "today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from you." The paronomasia is explicit: galoti (I have rolled) gives the name Gilgal (rolling/wheel). The cherpa (reproach/shame) of Egypt is the condition of being an uncircumcised, landless, slave people — now removed. In the cultural context of the ancient Near East, the lack of a settled inheritance was a source of shame; the land-entry removes it.
10: vayyachanu vnei Yisrael baGilgal vaya'asu et haPesach be'arba'ah asar yom lachodesh — Passover on the fourteenth day of the first month, in the plains of Jericho. The timing is covenant-precise: the first Passover in the land, forty years after the first Passover in Egypt. The dates bracket the wilderness period: Passover exits Egypt, Passover enters Canaan. The manna-to-produce transition follows (Joshua 5:11-12), completing the structural reversal from Exodus.
11: vayyokelu me'avur ha'arets mimochorat haPesach matsot uqali — "the day after the Passover they ate unleavened bread and roasted grain from the produce of the land." Me'avur ha'arets — from the produce/yield of the land. The word avur (produce) is connected to avor (to cross over) — they eat the fruit of the land they have just crossed into. The transition from manna to land-produce marks the completion of the wilderness phase.
12: vayishbot haman mimochorat — "the manna ceased the next day." Shavat — stopped/rested — the same verbal root as Shabbat. The manna's cessation is its Sabbath: having served its purpose for forty years, it rests. Ve'od lo hayah livnei Yisrael man vayyokelu mitvu'at erets Kena'an — "there was no more manna for the sons of Israel; they ate from the produce of the land of Canaan." Wilderness sustenance gives way to land sustenance; the grace of the desert becomes the grace of the promised inheritance.
13: vayyehi bihyot Yehoshua biYricho vayissa einav vayar vehineh ish omed lenegdo vecherbo shelufah beyado — "there was a man standing before him with his drawn sword in his hand." The drawn sword (cherbo shelufah) is the posture of a warrior ready for combat — neither sheathed nor used. The ambiguity of his stance (neither attacking nor retreating) frames Joshua's question: "Are you for us or against us?" The question assumes the man is human; the answer reveals he is not.
14: vayyomer lo ani sar-tseva YHWH atta vati — "as commander of the army of the LORD I have now come." Sar-tseva YHWH — commander of YHWH's army. The divine army (tseva) is the heavenly host; this is not an Israelite officer but the leader of the angelic armies. His "I have come" (atta vati) is an arrival announcement, not a recruitment pledge — he does not join Israel's mission; Israel joins his. Vayipol Yehoshua el panav artsah vayishtachu — Joshua fell facedown and worshiped, recognizing the divine character of the encounter.
15: shal na'alekha me'al raglekha ki hamaqom asher atta omed alav qadosh hu — "take off your sandal from your foot, for the place where you are standing is holy." The command directly echoes Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:5) — word for word. The holiness of the ground (qadosh) is the ground's proximity to divine presence. Vaya'as Yehoshua ken — "Joshua did so." The terse compliance mirrors Moses's response; Joshua is being consciously invested with Moses's role. The one who stood at the burning bush is now standing before Joshua at the threshold of conquest — the same divine presence, the same demand of reverent awe.
